tickets.title: §23.9/§22.6 — cap the anonymous render fan, and make the resolver's declared type bind
tickets.body:
BUILD-READY, NON-DESIGN. Audit 2's executable-code-at-rest angle verified most of the surface CLEAN (data-URL framing cannot break out; cache keying is a content address; the worker timeout genuinely cannot wedge the main loop; no authority crosses postMessage; pins resolve by content address at serve time; the browser-bundle invariant test would really catch its regression). Two defects survived, unrelated to each other but both small and both in the executable-code surface — build them together or split; they share no code.

1. (MED) THE ANONYMOUS RENDER FAN IS UNCAPPED. Every rendered-route hit spawns a fresh worker_threads Worker with NO concurrency gate (src/gateway/render-worker.ts:66-97, `new Worker(...)` unconditional, resourceLimits up to 128MB old + 32MB young, 500ms lifetime), and the route is reachable ANONYMOUSLY with an attacker-chosen entity (src/server/http.ts:616-624). N concurrent anonymous requests force N live V8 isolates/OS threads holding up to ~160MB each — thread and memory exhaustion of the host. THE CODEBASE'S OWN STANDARD SAYS THIS SHOULD BE BOUNDED: the sibling anonymous SSE door caps in-flight work with `maxPublicStreams` (src/server/http.ts:313, default 256), and a render is STRICTLY more expensive per request than a stream (it spawns an OS thread). THIS IS NOT THE DOCUMENTED RESIDUAL: §23.9's accepted residual is 'a worker bounds hang/mem but is not ocap (no-fs/no-net)', and its 'warm pool' note concerns per-render SPAWN COST, not a concurrency CAP. FIX: an in-flight cap on concurrent renders, modeled on `maxPublicStreams` (same option shape, same refusal register, a sane default); over the cap, refuse cleanly (a 503-shaped refusal that leaks nothing) rather than queueing unboundedly. Decide and STATE whether the cap is global or per-door; the SSE cap is public-door-scoped — follow that precedent unless there is a reason not to, and say why in the comment. RAILS: the cap refuses the (N+1)th CONCURRENT render while N are genuinely in flight, and the host survives; an under-cap load still serves normally; a completed render RELEASES its slot (the leak-the-slot bug is the obvious way to get this wrong — rail it: run cap+1 renders SEQUENTIALLY and assert every one succeeds).

2. (MED) A RESOLVER'S DECLARED TYPE DOES NOT BIND, SO TWO DOORS DISAGREE. `applyResolvers` writes `fn(entries)` straight into the view with NO check that the returned shape matches the resolver's declared `type` (src/gateway/resolvers.ts:151-179, the unvalidated write at :170-176). §22.6's provenance EXPLICITLY promises this typing 'keep[s] the §17 two-doors-agree invariant' — the invariant is advertised and never enforced. SEQUENCE: an operator registers a rung-(a) resolver on field f declared `{ rung: 'a', type: 'string', code }` whose ESM returns an OBJECT. GraphQL types f as String (`resolverTypeOf`, src/gateway/gql.ts:192-214), `GraphQLString.serialize(object)` THROWS -> the field nulls with a serialize error. REST/`nodeBody` runs the same view through bytesEnvelope + JSON.stringify and emits the object VERBATIM (src/surface/rest.ts:258+). Same registration, same entity: GraphQL answers null+error, REST answers the object — a concrete §17 two-doors-DISAGREE, precisely the invariant §22.6 claims to preserve. (v1 runs only the OPERATOR'S own resolvers, so this is a stated-invariant break and a footgun, not a privilege breach — hence MED.) FIX: validate the resolver's return against its declared ResolverOutputType at the apply seam, ONCE, where every door inherits it — and on a mismatch do exactly what a THROWING resolver already does (fall back to the Policy value, per-field, blast radius of one field: resolvers.ts:170-176 already has that discipline — reuse it, do not invent a second failure mode). Include the `bytes` type (§23.7's envelope shape) in the validation. RAILS: a resolver declaring `string` and returning an object falls back per-field AND BOTH DOORS AGREE (assert the SAME answer from GraphQL and REST for the same entity — that is the invariant, assert it directly); each declared type accepts its own valid value; one bad resolver does not take down the view or the door.

DO NOT: build the warm worker pool (a performance slice, named and separate); attempt the no-fs/no-net ocap (§23.9's documented residual, its own large work); widen resolver rungs beyond (a) (§22's ladder is decided); change the throwing-resolver fallback semantics (reuse them).

SPEC: spec/23-renderers.md gains the render cap in §23.9's honest-scope paragraph (the residual list currently names the hang and the ocap; the CAP is now built — say so). spec/22-resolvers.md §22.6: the two-doors-agree claim becomes true — note WHEN it became true and what enforces it. Both are corrections to built sections (edit in place, implementation note on the landing PR).

VILLAGE: the render cap wants an act that hammers a public route and shows the door refusing cleanly while the host stays up.

MIGRATION: none — no delta changes shape.

MERGE: item 1 touches an anonymous-door resource discipline and item 2 an executable-code surface -> Myk's merge (P6).

DONE WHEN: the cap refuses the over-limit concurrent render and releases slots correctly; a mistyped resolver falls back and both doors agree; `npm run check` green with the count read aloud; both spec sections tell the truth.
